sections.title: Here Comes the Rain!
sections.content_summary: The section opens with children observing the sky over a week to guess whether it will rain, drawing on clues from nature and elder knowledge. Students collect rainwater in a vessel and note whether it looks clean or dirty. They draw their observations of rainfall — its intensity, direction, size of drops — as a primary sensory record.

tasks.task_text: I think it will rain today because … (write at least 2 clues); I think it will not rain today because … (write at least 2 clues)

Ask yourself as many questions as you can about the rain: Is it a heavy rain or a light one? Are the raindrops big or small? Do they come down fast or slow? Is the rain so heavy that you do not see the water drops, but only lines or sheets of water? Is the rain falling straight down? Is it slanting or changing its direction? Guess why. Collect the rainwater in a vessel. Does it look clean or dirty?
Draw a picture of the rain that you observed.

sections.title: What happened to the rainwater?
sections.content_summary: This section explores the journey of rainwater after it falls — absorption into soil, flow into streams and rivers, collection in puddles, ponds and lakes, and storage as groundwater that feeds wells. Students imagine what happens on different surfaces and are prompted to observe local water bodies. The section grounds the water cycle at a concrete, observable level without introducing the formal cycle term.

tasks.task_text: Do you think it will rain again today? Or tomorrow? Why do you think so?
Did it rain only in your area or in other nearby places as well? Make a guess, then ask and find out.
Find some songs about the rain or make up one, in your language or any other. Sing some songs about the rain.
Did the rain make you happy? Did it make everyone happy?

Is there a stream, river, pond or lake near your village or city? Find out its name and some information about it. Visit it and draw its picture. Draw any trees, birds or animals that you see around it.
Find a poem about a river and sing it in the class.

sections.title: Water is a Precious Gift!
sections.content_summary: The section follows Surya and Barkha tracing where tap water comes from — rooftop tanks, pipes, wells, borewells, water tankers — and emphasises that people in many places must carry water from distant sources. Students are asked to appreciate those who bring them water. Traditional water storage vessels are introduced (clay pot, clay surahi, copper vessel, brass pot, steel pot, aluminium pot, glass bottle, plastic bottle, plastic bucket), reflecting an IKS thread on diverse material-based storage practices before modern pipes and taps existed.

tasks.task_text: Where do you get water from? Do you get water from a tap? If yes, ask the elders in your home from where the water comes into the tap.

Does someone bring the water to your home from a tap or a well or from a river or lake some distance away? Who carries this water? How do they bring it? Say a big 'thank you' to the people who bring you the water!
Do you get water delivered in a tanker? If yes, find out where the tankers bring water from. Does your water come from a well, river or lake near your village or city?
Do you know anyone who faces difficulty in getting water every day?
Ask your grandparents or any elder: How did they get water? Did they use water in the same way as you do? How did they store water? Write a short story about what you found out.

sections.title: Water in Our Daily Lives
sections.content_summary: Students list all activities requiring water and estimate water usage for tasks like brushing teeth or showering, developing a sense of water quantity. The Storing Water comic introduces why households store water in advance (pipeline bursts, irregular supply). Activity 3 directs students to identify vessels used at home, interview elders about historical vessels, draw and exhibit them, reflecting the IKS tradition of diverse water storage craft.

tasks.task_text: List all the activities you can think of for which we need water.
How many mugs of water do you need to brush your teeth? How much water do you use to take a shower?

Put up an exhibition: Identify different vessels used to keep water in your home. Ask the elders in your family what types of vessels they stored water in. Were they different from what we use now? Did the vessels have different names? On a separate sheet of paper draw a picture of any vessel that you find interesting and write its name in your language. Put up an exhibition of these pictures in your classroom with the help of your teacher. Walk around and observe the drawings by your classmates. How many different kinds of vessels have been drawn? Did you find any patterns on the vessels?

sections.title: Every Drop Counts
sections.content_summary: This section frames water as sacred and essential for survival, noting that in water-scarce areas people walk long distances to fetch it. The IKS tradition of keeping pots of water outside homes for passersby — especially in hot summer months — is highlighted as a cultural conservation practice. Activity 4 directs students to build a birdbath, a concrete act of water stewardship. The Let us Reflect section consolidates learning through writing, drawing, and group discussion on water pollution and conservation.

tasks.task_text: Prepare a birdbath — offer water to birds in the hot summer months. Take a shallow and wide container (an old big bowl or a base of an old pot or a bucket, less than 10 cm deep). Keep some stones in it for birds to perch. Add fresh water and keep the bowl in your yard, balcony or terrace. Change the water frequently. Clean the birdbath with a scrubber twice a week.

You waited for rain and watched how raindrops fell to the ground. You collected rainwater and observed if it was clean or dirty. You saw what happened to the rainwater that fell in different places. Now write a few lines on your observations of the rain.

You found out the name and some information about a stream, river, well, pond or lake near your place. Write it in a few lines: Is this water used for drinking? If yes, how is this water carried to your home? If it is not used, why not? Was it used in the past?
Draw a picture of your birdbath. Write the names of the birds and insects that come to drink water from your birdbath.

Water is very precious. We should be cautious about how we use it. We should not waste even a drop of water. Discuss among yourselves and list the activities in your house or outside, due to which water gets polluted or wasted. How can we avoid wastage of water? Think of three solutions with your group and write them in your notebook.

dual_strand.natural: Rainfall as the source of water — its journey from rain to groundwater, streams, rivers, wells, and household taps
dual_strand.human_cultural: Traditional water storage vessels, community water-sharing practices (pots outside homes), water conservation and reuse
